# 🇱🇰 Sri Lanka This Week

![LastUpdated](https://img.shields.io/badge/last_updated-2026--05--01_06:59:27-green)
[![RSS](https://img.shields.io/badge/RSS-Feed-orange)](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nuuuwan/lk_news_digest/refs/heads/main/rss.xml)

*Generated by gpt-5 from 158 English News Articles published between 2026-04-24 and 2026-05-01.*

## 1. Sri Lanka’s system operator invited international bids to upgrade the National System Control Centre under a power strengthening programme.

The procuring authority specified integration of a renewable energy management layer with a modernised SCADA/EMS platform, using open competitive bidding rules backed by multilateral financing. Prequalification criteria set a minimum single‑contract track record above USD 6 million and a three‑year average turnover over USD 16 million, indicating a field of large engineering vendors and an 18‑month implementation window from award that would align with grid‑intelligence needs as variable generation rises toward policy targets.

## 2. Procurement reductions and shipment delays cut contracted coal volumes for the current supply season, increasing power‑sector exposure.

Revised call‑offs trimmed expected deliveries by roughly 160,000 tonnes from an original plan of 2.32 million tonnes, even as burn rates rose by more than a tenth due to plant and fuel characteristics, implying higher tonnes per kilowatt‑hour than in 2020–2025. Slippage in vessel schedules would force greater reliance on oil‑fired units, which historically lifted generation costs and foreign‑exchange use when hydrology weakened and global fuel prices spiked, as seen during the 2022 emergency when thermal shares climbed toward half of output.

## 3. A 100 MW utility‑scale solar project secured a local‑currency loan of up to $18 million from an international financier.

Annual generation of about 220 GWh would add roughly 1.5% to a 2025 demand base near 15,000 GWh while creating more than 3,000 jobs across construction and operations, a contribution comparable to mid‑sized thermal peakers in energy terms but without import dependence. The structure leveraged concessional resources to de‑risk currency exposure and is aligned with a policy trajectory that targeted 50–70% renewables by 2030, complementing grid‑integration investments supported under broader World Bank Group programmes.

## 4. A domestic bank won the Innovation – Use of Proceeds (Green Bond) award at Environmental Finance’s Sustainable Debt Awards 2026 for a listed blue bond.

The instrument, issued in November 2025 for LKR 3 billion—about USD 10 million at the time—was cross‑listed on offshore platforms and earmarked for water and marine priorities such as pollution prevention, ecosystem restoration and coastal adaptation, with measurable indicators attached. As the market’s first of its kind locally, it expanded the sustainable‑debt toolkit beyond land‑based themes and set a replicable framework for island economies where coastal assets underpin a large share of GDP.

## 5. Parliament’s finance committee approved new regulations easing local purchases at duty‑free outlets in Colombo Port City and revised exemptions under the Termination of Employment Act.

Officials reported that foreign footfall in the special zone accounted for only about 1.5% of national visitor traffic, hence wider domestic access was cleared alongside leakage monitoring to support retail viability. Separately, prior blanket exclusions for strategically important enterprises under TEWA were narrowed to three sections, leaving core protections intact while removing some pre‑approval bottlenecks for terminations—an adjustment intended to balance labour safeguards with investment agility.

## 6. An insurance‑led group completed the largest initial public offering on the CSE in 14 years, raising over Rs. 5 billion.

The book closed with oversubscriptions and an investor base exceeding 20,000 accounts, a breadth last seen before the 2012–2015 lull in primary equity issuance. Although risk appetite softened in the weeks around pricing due to oil‑market turbulence and regional conflict, pre‑marketing anchored allocations within listing‑rule parameters, adding fresh float to a market where annual IPO proceeds had been modest relative to GDP for much of the past decade.

## 7. Merchandise shipments of garments fell 8% year‑on‑year in the first quarter of 2026.

Monthly prints showed a 3% dip in January and double‑digit contractions in both February and March, with consignments to the top buyer market—about 40% of the portfolio—down slightly less than the aggregate and the European bloc posting a similar decline. Elevated electricity and fuel inputs added nearly USD 3 million a month to industry costs, sharpening the case for accelerated open‑access and wheeling reforms, while a temporary 10% levy under Section 122 for 150 days and ongoing Section 301 reviews in the United States created a defined window to negotiate parity with peer exporters ahead of the 2027 GSP+ decision in Europe.

## 8. A remittance of USD 2.5 million intended for a sovereign obligation was misdirected to an unauthorised account during processing.

Early reporting pointed to an email‑based alteration of beneficiary details rather than a core‑system breach, underscoring gaps in call‑back protocols that should have flagged changes to verified payment instructions for cross‑border government transfers. The incident, which triggered street demonstrations and calls for a legislative inquiry, was material in reputational terms despite being small relative to past‑year external service cash flows, and it highlighted the need for independent forensic reviews and stronger segregation of duties in high‑value workflows.

## 9. A listed bank reported an estimated Rs. 13.2 billion fraud and accepted the resignation of an independent director during April 2026.

Internal investigations indicated that a small group exploited ledger, suspense and interbank settlement processes over an extended period, prompting an external forensic mandate and enhanced oversight by the prudential authority. The institution guided to a quarterly after‑tax loss near Rs. 4 billion as provisions were raised—an earnings swing that, at sector level, revived debate about audit effectiveness, board risk literacy and whistleblowing mechanisms after a decade in which finance made up a large share of market capitalisation.

## 10. The credit bureau introduced temporary reporting concessions for borrowers affected by Cyclone Ditwah, effective from 1 March 2026.

For up to six months, arrears arising from disaster‑linked cash‑flow stress were not classified as overdue and new facilities for restarting operations were tagged as recovery credit, with normal reporting resuming if servicing ceased thereafter. As the first such entity in South Asia—established by statute in 1990—the bureau’s measures aimed to preserve access for households and SMEs in districts where November–December 2025 floods destroyed over 6,000 homes and partially damaged more than 115,000, according to later government tallies.

## 11. The Government issued interim digital guidelines to expand remote working across public institutions amid energy and fuel constraints.

The circular clarified that electronic documents, digital signatures and official email under the Electronic Transactions Act carried legal effect, and recommended use of the State video platform for virtual meetings while allowing other tools as needed. By formalising hybrid workflows and digitised approvals, the move sought to moderate transport and building loads during periods when liquidity‑driven rate cuts had lowered the prime lending benchmark to 9.63% and system surplus balances approached Rs. 200 billion, signalling scope to reallocate savings toward service delivery.
